perturbation.original_text: Turpin acknowledges and agrees that all Work Product have been made for the Company and that the Company shall be the exclusive owner of all right, title, and interest in and to the Work Product and all Intellectual Property Rights therein. Turpin does hereby assign and transfer to the Company, effective upon creation, all right, title, and interest that Turpin may have in and to the Work Product and all Intellectual Property Rights therein and does hereby assign all of Turpin's future right, title, and interest that Turpin may have in and to each of the Work Product and Intellectual Property Rights therein, effective at the time each is created. Turpin hereby irrevocably and expressly waives as against any person all moral rights Turpin may have in any and all Work Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Turpin hereby irrevocably and expressly waives as against any person all moral rights Turpin may have in any and all Work Product. Turpin acknowledges and agrees that all Work Product have been made for the Company and that the Company shall be the exclusive owner of all right, title, and interest in and to the Work Product and all Intellectual Property Rights therein. Turpin does hereby assign and transfer to the Company, effective upon creation, all right, title, and interest that Turpin may have in and to the Work Product and all Intellectual Property Rights therein and does hereby assign all of Turpin's future right, title, and interest that Turpin may have in and to each of the Work Product and Intellectual Property Rights therein, effective at the time each is created.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the waiver of moral rights to the beginning of the paragraph obfuscates its significance, making it less clear that Turpin is intentionally relinquishing these rights. This could be seen as a structural flaw because important rights waivers are usually explicitly addressed at the conclusion of the paragraph.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Copyright Act, RSC 1985, c C-42, s 14.2
perturbation.law_explanation: The moved text buries the moral rights waiver, which in Canada, requires explicit renunciation as per RSC 1985, c C-42, s 14.2. Placing it at the beginning of the paragraph instead of emphasizing it may hinder its clear and informed renunciation, making it harder to demonstrate that Turpin fully understood and agreed to waive these rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Copyright Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Term 14.2 Â (1) Â Moral rights in respect of a work subsist for the same term as the copyright in the work. Marginal note: Succession (2) Â The moral rights in respect of a work pass, on the death of its author, to (a) Â the person to whom those rights are specifically bequeathed; (b) Â where there is no specific bequest of those moral rights and the author dies testate in respect of the copyright in the work, the person to whom that copyright is bequeathed; or (c) Â where there is no person described in paragraph (a) or (b), the person entitled to any other property in respect of which the author dies intestate. Marginal note: Subsequent succession (3) Â Subsection (2) applies, with such modifications as the circumstances require, on the death of any person who holds moral rights. R.S., 1985, c. 10 (4th Supp.), s. 4 1997, c. 24, s. 13 Table of Contents 1 ... 2 2.1 ... 6.1 6.2 ... 15 16 ... 20 21 ... 27 27.1 ... 29.4 29.5 ... 30.03 30.04 ... 30.6 30.61 ... 31.1 32 ... 32.5 32.6 ... 38.1 38.2 ... 41.13 41.14 ... 41.25 41.26 ... 44.01 44.02 ... 44.12 44.2 ... 57 58 ... 66.1 66.2 ... 68 68.1 ... 72 72.1 ... 79 80 ... 88 89 ... 92 SCHEDULE I : Existing Rights SCHEDULE II SCHEDULE III Related Provisions Amendments not in force Previous Amendments ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-06-12 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that moving the moral rights waiver to the beginning of the paragraph obfuscates its significance. The law explanation states that under RSC 1985, c C-42, s 14.2, explicit renunciation is required, and placing the waiver at the beginning may hinder its clear and informed renunciation. The scraped law snippet 1, which cites the Copyright Act, Marginal note: Term 14.2 Â (1) Â, relates to moral rights. While snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state where this waiver should be placed, the move weakens the clarity and emphasis and creates a structural flaw, affecting enforceability based on its lack of clarity.

perturbation.original_text: Time shall be of the essence for each and every term and condition hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Time shall be of the essence for some terms and conditions hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'each and every' to 'some' introduces ambiguity regarding which terms 'time is of the essence' applies to. This introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Sale of Goods Act, RSBC 1996, c 410, s 11
perturbation.law_explanation: As per the Sale of Goods Act, where time is of the essence, failure to comply may allow the non-breaching party to repudiate the contract. Time of the essence being only applicable to 'some' terms weakens the impact of this as per RSBC 1996, c 410, s 11.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'each and every' to 'some' introduces ambiguity. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this ambiguity creates uncertainty. The Law Explanation states that 'time of the essence being only applicable to 'some' terms weakens the impact' of the Sale of Goods Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that section 14 refers to "Stipulations as to time." By making the 'time is of the essence' clause apply only to 'some' terms, the contract weakens the enforceability of specific time-related obligations, creating a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction.
